GOOG.title: 5 Stocks Investors Couldn't Stop Buzzing About This Week: AAPL, GOOG, HOOD And More

macro_events.event_summary: Major US oil companies are reporting lower earnings than before the Iran war, suggesting crude prices have fallen back despite geopolitical risk premium expectations.
primary_drivers: Lower realized crude/refined product prices compressing upstream margins
Cost inflation (services, labor) not receding alongside revenue

beneficiaries.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of airline operating costs; sustained lower crude directly expands margins. Delta has been most vocal about fuel cost sensitivity in guidance.
Same fuel-cost mechanic as DAL; United has higher long-haul exposure where fuel is an even larger cost share.
Lower naphtha/ethane-linked feedstock costs improve chemicals spreads; Dow's commodity chemicals segment is highly sensitive to oil-derived input costs.

losers.rationale: Directly named cohort — upstream price realizations down with crude, and downstream/chemicals not offsetting. Earnings compression is the story.
Same upstream realization pressure as XOM; Permian and Gulf production economics weaker at current strip than pre-conflict assumptions.
Pure-play E&P with no downstream buffer — most direct earnings sensitivity to lower crude realizations among majors.
High leverage post-Anadarko/CrownRock makes cash-flow sensitive to crude prices; lower realized prices pressure debt-paydown thesis.
Oilfield services demand and pricing power tied to E&P capex, which is set off operator cash flows — sustained margin compression at majors threatens 2026 service budgets.
Same E&P capex transmission as SLB; North American completion activity most exposed to majors' budget cuts.
Refining crack spreads referenced in the same earnings cycle — weak downstream contribution at majors implies sector-wide refining margin pressure.

headlines: America’s biggest oil companies are making less money than before the Iran war

macro_events.event_summary: Japan's recent yen intervention is seen as a temporary fix that could be undone by rising oil prices, since Japan imports nearly all its energy and higher crude widens its trade deficit, pressuring the yen lower again.
primary_drivers: Yen weakness driven by Japan's energy import bill (oil priced in USD)
FX translation headwinds for US companies with large Japan revenue exposure
Japanese exporter competitiveness vs US peers when yen weakens

beneficiaries.rationale: Honda ADR — weaker yen boosts JPY-denominated earnings when translated, and improves export margins on US-bound vehicles built in Japan. Direct beneficiary of the thesis that yen intervention fails.

Toyota ADR — same mechanism as Honda but larger scale; ~25%+ of vehicles sold in US are Japan-built, so a weaker yen directly expands operating margin per unit. Most liquid US-listed pure-play on yen weakness.
If the article's premise (oil staying high) holds, integrated oil majors with upstream leverage capture the windfall; XOM is the most liquid US pure-play on sustained crude prices.

losers.rationale: Caterpillar competes directly with Komatsu in construction/mining equipment; a sustained weaker yen hands Komatsu a 5-10% pricing advantage in third-country export markets, pressuring CAT's international margins.
Deere competes with Kubota in compact ag/turf equipment globally; weaker yen improves Kubota's relative pricing in US and emerging markets, modest market-share headwind.

headlines: Japan just put a ‘Band-Aid’ on the yen. Why high oil prices could soon rip it off.
